findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that formalizes the Teeth check, e.g., 'A one-line perturbation to `JiraBackend` (drop the priority field from the outbound map) causes at least one contract or characterization test to go RED — verified by introducing the perturbation, running `make test`, observing failure, then reverting.'
evidence: Testing section states: 'Teeth: perturb a JiraBackend delegation (e.g. drop the priority map) and confirm a contract/characterization test goes RED, then restore.'
ABSENCE: none of the five ACs references this mutation step or requires evidence that at least one test goes RED when a delegation is dropped
findings.finding: The 'Teeth' mutation-test step (perturb a JiraBackend delegation, confirm RED, restore) is described in the Testing section but has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion, so there is no proving command that verifies the contract suite would actually catch a regression.
findings.location: Testing section ('Teeth' bullet) / Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
scenarios: An executor writes characterization tests that trivially pass because they assert on a mock return value rather than the actual mapper output; the Teeth step would catch this but it is unverifiable without an AC
A reviewer completing this story cannot confirm the mutation was performed

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that explicitly covers the 'Teeth' test: e.g., 'A commit that drops one `JiraBackend` delegation (e.g. the priority field mapping) causes at least one characterization or contract test to go RED — verified by a named test that asserts the perturbation scenario.'
evidence: Testing section: 'Teeth: perturb a `JiraBackend` delegation (e.g. drop the priority map) and confirm a contract/characterization test goes RED, then restore.'
No AC references 'teeth', perturbation, or RED confirmation for a deliberate delegation break.
findings.finding: The Testing section describes a mandatory 'Teeth' step — 'perturb a `JiraBackend` delegation (e.g. drop the priority map) and confirm a contract/characterization test goes RED, then restore' — but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this was performed, leaving it as undisprovable guidance rather than a checkable gate.
findings.location: Testing section ('Teeth' paragraph) — no corresponding AC
scenarios: Implementer writes passing tests without ever verifying the test suite has teeth; the 'teeth' check is skipped and no AC gate catches the omission.

findings.finding: The 'Teeth' process step — 'perturb a JiraBackend delegation (e.g. drop the priority map) and confirm a contract/characterization test goes RED, then restore' — is described work in the Testing section with no corresponding acceptance criterion that verifies it was performed.
findings.location: Testing section, 'Teeth' subsection; Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `IdentityConvention.parse(label_str)` reconstructs `vendor` and `instance` fields of `RemoteRef` (e.g., via constructor-injected constants at `JiraBackend` construction time), given that the label format `rebar-id:{remote_id}` encodes only `remote_id`.
evidence: Plan states: 'formats a RemoteRef → the rebar-id:{remote_id} label string and parses that label back to a RemoteRef'
Codebase confirms label format encodes only remote_id: binding_store.py:522 `colon_label = f"rebar-id:{local_id}"`, binding_walk.py:341 `for prefix in ("rebar-id:", "rebar-id-"):`
RemoteRef has three fields: vendor, instance, remote_id — only remote_id appears in the label encoding
Contract test requires 'round-trip' but a true round-trip is impossible without a specified rule for recovering vendor/instance (e.g., constructor-injected constants or a fixed parse fallback)
findings.finding: The `IdentityConvention` Protocol is specified to both stamp (`RemoteRef → label string`) and parse (`label string → RemoteRef`), but the label format encodes only `remote_id` (confirmed in codebase: `rebar-id:{local_id}`), making `vendor` and `instance` unrecoverable from the label string alone. The rule for reconstructing those `RemoteRef` fields during parse is never specified.
findings.location: Approach section — IdentityConvention description; Testing section — 'parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip'
scenarios: Implementer writes `IdentityConvention.parse('rebar-id:abc123')` and has no specified source for `vendor` and `instance` fields of the returned `RemoteRef` — must guess whether to use hard-coded 'jira' string, a constructor argument, or raise
The contract test round-trip assertion `parse(stamp(ref)) == ref` fails unless vendor/instance are preserved, but they are not encoded in the label
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify that `IdentityConvention` is constructed with `vendor` and `instance` as fixed constants (e.g., `JiraIdentityConvention(vendor='jira', instance=<instance_url>)`), so `parse` uses those injected values to fill the non-encoded `RemoteRef` fields, and document this in the Protocol's method signature.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the method names and signatures for each of the three opt-in capability Protocols (`SupportsLinks`, `SupportsComments`, `SupportsIncremental`) in the plan, derived from the existing call sites (`outbound_links._diff_links`, `outbound_comments._diff_comments`, and any incremental-fetch surface), so `FakeBackend`, `JiraBackend`, and the contract test all target the same interface.
evidence: Plan states: 'the three opt-in capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental)' are `@runtime_checkable`
Plan states: 'callers detect an opt-in capability with isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks) / isinstance(backend, SupportsComments) / isinstance(backend, SupportsIncremental)'
No method signatures are listed for any of the three capability Protocols anywhere in the plan
For `@runtime_checkable` Protocols, `isinstance()` checks pass for ANY object if the Protocol has no required methods — making the contract vacuous without at least one named method per Protocol
findings.finding: The three opt-in capability Protocols (`SupportsLinks`, `SupportsComments`, `SupportsIncremental`) are named and declared `@runtime_checkable`, but their method surfaces (the method names and signatures a backend must implement to satisfy the `isinstance()` check) are never enumerated in the plan.
findings.location: Context / Problem section — capability Protocol list; _backend.py edit-set description
scenarios: Implementer must guess what methods `SupportsLinks` requires (e.g., `sync_links()`? `create_link()`? `list_links()`?), leading to inconsistent implementations across `JiraBackend` and `FakeBackend`
Contract test that exercises 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it' cannot be written without knowing which methods to call on the Protocol

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate ALL rebar-id-label formatting call sites (including binding_store.py:522-523 and dispatch_one.py:237's JQL embedding) in the IdentityConvention design, and decide whether the Protocol needs a `jql_clause(ref) -> str` method alongside stamp/parse.
evidence: Plan states: 'the single source of the format those four sites currently hand-inline' — but grep finds: dispatch_one.py:237 `jql = f'labels = "rebar-id:{local_id}"'`, binding_store.py:522 `colon_label = f"rebar-id:{local_id}"`, binding_store.py:527 `hyphen_label = f"rebar-id-{local_id}"` (legacy fallback also not enumerated)
S2 creates IdentityConvention with stamp/parse methods; S4 routes all call sites through it — missing sites mean S4's edit-set is under-specified
findings.finding: The plan enumerates 'four core call sites' for the rebar-id label convention (dispatch_one.py:320, binding_store.py:516, apply_inbound_records.py:223, binding_walk.py:341), but grep reveals at least two additional label-formatting sites: binding_store.py:522-523 (formats 'rebar-id:{local_id}' as both a label string and a JQL clause) and dispatch_one.py:237 (formats 'rebar-id:{local_id}' inside a JQL string). The IdentityConvention object introduced by S2 is described as covering stamp→label and parse←label, but the JQL-embedding call sites are not mentioned. If S4 routes all rebar-id: formatting through IdentityConvention, the interface as designed may need a jql_clause(ref) method — omitting these sites now risks a gap when S4 attempts full routing.
findings.location: Approach section — 'IdentityConvention' paragraph; binding_store.py:522-523, dispatch_one.py:237
scenarios: S4 attempts to route all rebar-id: call sites through IdentityConvention and discovers binding_store.py:522 and dispatch_one.py:237 also format the label — requiring either a jql_clause method on IdentityConvention or a separate design decision not captured in S2.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the IdentityConvention description: 'also covers `jql_clause(ref) -> str` returning the JQL-safe label predicate (e.g. `labels = "rebar-id:{remote_id}"`)' and update the call-site inventory from 4 to 6.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the correct home for `fit_text_to_adf_limit` in the Protocol surface: it is called by `_diff_fields` (the comparison path in `outbound_fields.py:567`), not by `_map_local_to_jira_fields` (the mapping path); either move it to the correct role Protocol or justify its inclusion in `OutboundMapper` with a concrete call-site reference.
evidence: Full read of outbound_fields.py:112-198 (_map_local_to_jira_fields): no call to fit_text_to_adf_limit
Search of outbound_fields.py for 'fit_text_to_adf': zero matches
outbound_fields.py:567: 'local_val = _load_adf().fit_text_to_adf_limit(local_val)' — this is inside _diff_fields, not _map_local_to_jira_fields
Prior cache finding .rebar/cache/plan-review/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/ac84a39baed648ecbb0c.json also flags this same mismatch
findings.finding: The plan attributes `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` to `OutboundMapper` ('OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'), but a full read of `_map_local_to_jira_fields` (outbound_fields.py:112-198) and a search of outbound_fields.py for 'fit_text_to_adf' confirm the function is never called there. `fit_text_to_adf_limit` is called from `_diff_fields` (outbound_fields.py:567) — the field-comparison path, not the mapping path. Attributing it to OutboundMapper is a false premise: the Protocol's method surface derived from the de-facto call sites would not include `fit_text_to_adf_limit` under the mapping role.
findings.location: Approach section — 'OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'
scenarios: JiraBackend's OutboundMapper implementation would either duplicate the ADF-truncation call (also present in _diff_fields) or omit it from the Protocol method, producing an OutboundMapper that doesn't reflect the actual de-facto surface the core calls.
Contract tests written against OutboundMapper's map method expecting ADF truncation behavior may never fire it via the Protocol path, giving false green.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `(+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)` from the `OutboundMapper` derivation in the Approach section. If ADF truncation must be part of the backend interface, assign it to the role Protocol whose de-facto surface includes `_diff_fields` (likely a combined OutboundMapper that wraps both map + diff, or a separate FieldNormalizer role), and update the contract test assertion accordingly.

findings.finding: The plan places `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` under `OutboundMapper` (`OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)`), but the actual codebase shows `fit_text_to_adf_limit` is NOT called inside `_map_local_to_jira_fields` — it is called from `_diff_fields` (the differ/comparison path at `outbound_fields.py:567`) and from `inbound_differ.py:192`. The plan's own stated derivation principle is to 'derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls', which would place `fit_text_to_adf_limit` in `FieldSanitizer` (already backed by `_sanitize_description`, which itself calls `fit_text_to_adf_limit`), not in `OutboundMapper`. Misattributing it to `OutboundMapper` would produce a Protocol that doesn't match actual call-site boundaries, complicating the S4 rewiring where sites call `fit_text_to_adf_limit` via the FieldSanitizer path (description send-side truncation) rather than the mapper path.
findings.location: Approach section — 'OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields (+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Approach bullet to: `OutboundMapper ← outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields` (remove `+ adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit`); add `fit_text_to_adf_limit` to `FieldSanitizer` alongside `_sanitize_description` (which already delegates to it), consistent with `adapters/jira/jira_fields.py:18` importing it for that purpose.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the `IdentityConvention` contract whether `parse()` must also accept the legacy hyphen-form (`rebar-id-{id}`) as a secondary parse path (matching `binding_walk.py:341`'s dual-prefix scan), and add a contract-test case for it so S4's rewire of `binding_walk.py` cannot silently drop legacy bindings.
evidence: Plan: 'IdentityConvention … formats a RemoteRef → the rebar-id:{remote_id} label string and parses that label back to a RemoteRef'
Plan contract test: 'stamp a RemoteRef → label string; parse label string → RemoteRef; round-trip' — no mention of legacy hyphen-form
binding_walk.py:341: `for prefix in ("rebar-id:", "rebar-id-"): if lbl.startswith(prefix) and lbl[len(prefix):].strip():`
binding_store.py:525-527: `hyphen_label = f"rebar-id-{local_id}"` fallback search for legacy issues
binding_walk.py comment: 'perpetual unbound_jira drift; the REB-659 case'
findings.finding: The `IdentityConvention` contract spec covers only the canonical colon-form (`rebar-id:{remote_id}`) but the live codebase also reads the legacy hyphen-form (`rebar-id-`) in `binding_walk.py:341` and `binding_store.py:525-527`. If S2 defines `IdentityConvention.parse()` to handle only the colon-form (what the contract test exercises), an implementer building S4 will find that routing `binding_walk.py` through `IdentityConvention` silently drops the legacy-prefix fallback, causing perpetual unbound_jira drift for pre-migration issues — the exact failure mode documented in the `binding_walk.py` comment about REB-659.
findings.location: Approach section — IdentityConvention paragraph; Testing section — 'IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef'
scenarios: S4 routes binding_walk.py through IdentityConvention.parse(); pre-migration Jira issues with rebar-id- labels are no longer recognized as bound, causing them to be treated as unbound and re-adopted on every reconciler pass.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section's IdentityConvention paragraph, add: 'IdentityConvention.parse() accepts both the canonical colon-form (`rebar-id:{id}`) and the legacy hyphen-form (`rebar-id-{id}`), returning a RemoteRef in both cases (matching binding_walk.py:341's dual-prefix scan). The contract test includes a parse case for each prefix.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the structural form of `Backend` in the plan (e.g., 'Backend is a @runtime_checkable Protocol composed of the five role Protocol method signatures' or 'Backend is an ABC'), so the executor has no design decision to make.
evidence: Plan names 'the `Backend` facade' and states 'JiraBackend implements `Backend`' but gives no structural specification (Protocol @runtime_checkable? ABC with abstractmethods? typing.Protocol with all five role method sets inlined?)
The five capability Protocols are explicitly called @runtime_checkable, but no such annotation is specified for Backend itself
ABSENCE: no sentence of the form 'Backend is a Protocol / ABC / class'
findings.finding: The `Backend` facade type is unspecified — the plan never states whether `Backend` is a Protocol, an ABC, or a plain class aggregating the five role Protocols. An executor must make a real design choice (Protocol vs ABC changes how JiraBackend must be declared and how isinstance checks work) with no guidance.
findings.location: Scope section / AC1: '`_backend.py` defines `Backend`…'
scenarios: Executor makes Backend a plain Protocol → isinstance(jira_backend, Backend) succeeds structurally even if delegation is broken
Executor makes Backend an ABC → JiraBackend must declare all abstract methods, changing the shim shape
Type-checker behavior differs between Protocol and ABC, making AC5 (make typecheck green) non-deterministic
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Scope bullet for `_backend.py`: 'Backend is itself a `@runtime_checkable Protocol` whose body is the union of the five role Protocol method signatures, so `isinstance(jira_backend, Backend)` works structurally and JiraBackend needs no explicit inheritance.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC4's file-list to account for any `__init__.py` or package-init additions (e.g., 'touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, any new `__init__.py` files required to make those modules importable, and files under `tests/`').
evidence: The plan introduces `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/jira/backend.py` as a new file in what may be an existing package — if the `adapters/jira/` directory already exists, no `__init__.py` is needed; but if any intermediate directory is new (e.g., `adapters/`), the executor would need to add one
ABSENCE: the plan does not state whether `adapters/jira/` already exists as a Python package, leaving the executor uncertain whether the git diff constraint is achievable as written
findings.finding: AC4's `git diff --name-only` constraint lists only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and `tests/` as touched files, but introducing `adapters/jira/` as a Python package likely requires a new `__init__.py` (or similar module-init file) in a non-test directory, which would violate the stated constraint.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: 'verified by `git diff --name-only` touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and files under `tests/`'
scenarios: Executor adds a required `__init__.py` and fails their own AC4 check
Executor omits `__init__.py` to satisfy AC4, breaking the import in AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC4: 'verified by `git diff --name-only` touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, any `__init__.py` files required to make those modules importable as packages, and files under `tests/`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the file-layout ambiguity for `IdentityConvention`: either explicitly state it lives inside `adapters/jira/backend.py` (and drop 'self-contained' language that implies its own module), or add `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py` (or the chosen filename) to AC4's `git diff` enumeration.
evidence: AC4: 'verified by `git diff --name-only` touching only `_backend.py`, `adapters/jira/backend.py`, and files under `tests/`'
Approach: 'S2 introduces `IdentityConvention` as a **new, self-contained pure convention object under `adapters/jira/`'
Edit-set lists only two new source files, no third file for IdentityConvention
findings.finding: AC4's git diff verification enumerates exactly two new source files (`_backend.py` and `adapters/jira/backend.py`), but the Approach section describes `IdentityConvention` as 'a new, self-contained pure convention object under `adapters/jira/`' — language that implies it may warrant its own module file. If an implementer reasonably creates `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py` (or similar), AC4's named-file check would spuriously fail, sending the implementer in two directions on file layout.
findings.location: AC4 (`git diff --name-only` check) vs. Approach section ('a new, self-contained pure convention object under `adapters/jira/`')
scenarios: Implementer creates a separate `adapters/jira/identity_convention.py` for the IdentityConvention class (reasonable given 'self-contained' language); AC4 git diff check fails even though the implementation is correct.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, replace the exact filename enumeration with a glob: 'touching only `_backend.py`, files under `adapters/jira/`, and files under `tests/`' — or explicitly state in the Approach that `IdentityConvention` is a class within `adapters/jira/backend.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Scope section: update ADR 0035 (or author a new ADR/design-doc section) to formally capture the `Backend` facade structure, the five role Protocols, the three `@runtime_checkable` capability Protocols with `isinstance`-guarded dispatch (and the explicit absence of a `capabilities()` method), and the `IdentityConvention` rationale — so these decisions survive beyond the story's PR description.
evidence: Plan cites 'RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} dataclass pinned in ADR 0035 §(d)' but names no ADR update for the Backend facade itself
'IdentityConvention … is not a delegation shim over existing core code' — a novel design decision with no ADR capture specified
Capability-detection mechanism described as 'pinned here' but only in plan prose, not in a living architectural document
No mention of updating AGENTS.md, ADR 0035, or creating a new ADR anywhere in the Scope or AC sections
findings.finding: The plan introduces a significant new architectural port — the `Backend` facade, five role Protocols, three `@runtime_checkable` capability Protocols, the capability-detection mechanism (isinstance-guarded dispatch with no `capabilities()` method), and `IdentityConvention` as a new self-contained object — but does not specify updating ADR 0035 or authoring any design doc to formally capture these decisions. ADR 0035 is cited only for `RemoteRef` (§(d)); the facade structure, Protocol hierarchy, and IdentityConvention rationale are nowhere memorialized.
findings.location: Context / Approach sections; AC item 1 (`_backend.py` import test)
scenarios: A future contributor implementing a second backend (epic `be74`) has no authoritative doc explaining why `isinstance`-guarded dispatch was chosen over a `capabilities()` method
ADR 0035 remains scoped only to `RemoteRef`, leaving the Backend/Protocol contract undocumented at the ADR level
If the IdentityConvention label format must change, engineers have no ADR to update — the design rationale is only in the story text
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, add an edit-set entry: update `docs/adr/0035-*.md` (or a new `docs/adr/0036-backend-port.md`) with: (1) the Backend facade interface and its role-Protocol composition; (2) the capability-detection contract (`@runtime_checkable` + `isinstance`, not `capabilities()`); (3) the `IdentityConvention` as the single source of the `rebar-id:` label format. Reference this ADR from `_backend.py`'s module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'captured from current behavior' snapshot oracles in the characterization tests with independently-specified expected values (hand-authored from the field-mapping specification or ADR), so the tests document the *intended* behavior rather than mirroring the code's current output.
evidence: 'Characterization tests pin the **exact current** outputs of the pure Jira mappers … for representative tickets'
'verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior (a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty)'
The oracle is generated by running the same code that the test will later call, making it definitionally a snapshot/change-detector.
findings.finding: The characterization tests are described as capturing 'literal expected values captured from current behavior' to assert 'byte-for-byte identical' outputs — this is the self-authored-oracle / change-detector anti-pattern: the oracle is derived from the code under test (the existing mapper functions called through JiraBackend), meaning any current bug in those mappers is locked into the test as a permanent expected value.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Characterization tests pin the exact current outputs … verified by literal expected values captured from current behavior'; AC line 3 — 'a diff of the captured vs. produced dict is empty'
scenarios: A latent bug in `_map_local_to_jira_fields` is captured as the expected value; the characterization test then permanently enshrines the wrong output, and any future fix causes the test to go RED rather than GREEN.
A reviewer cannot tell from the test whether the expected dict reflects a specification or a snapshot — the test loses documentary value.
findings.suggested_fix: For each representative fixture, hand-author the expected output dict from the field-mapping spec/ADR (e.g., what the Jira priority field *should* be for a given local priority). Use these spec-derived dicts as the oracle. Reserve the 'capture and diff' technique only as a one-time discovery tool during authoring, then replace the captured value with a human-reviewed, spec-grounded constant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `SupportsIncremental` is grounded in an existing or in-flight feature with ≥1 concrete method to delegate to; if no such feature exists yet, defer it to the story that introduces incremental fetching rather than landing an untestable stub Protocol here.
evidence: Grep for 'incremental' across src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler returns zero hits.
Grep for 'SupportsIncremental' returns zero hits in the codebase.

The plan's Approach section grounds TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, and IdentityConvention in existing call-sites, but gives no existing call-site for SupportsIncremental.
S4 (6673-7636-a116-4f90) lists 12 concrete rewire sites and none involves an incremental fetch path.

The Testing section says the contract suite tests capability opt-in/skip via isinstance dispatch — but there is no existing JiraBackend method body to put behind SupportsIncremental, and the contract test would be testing a stub with no behavior.
findings.finding: `SupportsIncremental` is a capability Protocol with zero existing call-sites, no current reconciler feature named 'incremental', and no story in the S2-S5 chain that wires a consumer to it — making it a premature abstraction that violates Rule-of-Three and YAGNI.
findings.location: Context / Problem section — edit-set for `_backend.py`; Testing section — 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it'
scenarios: Implementer must invent method signatures for SupportsIncremental with no existing logic to delegate to, risking an interface that doesn't match the eventual feature's needs.
The contract test for SupportsIncremental exercises a no-op stub, providing false confidence that the capability mechanism works end-to-end.
findings.suggested_fix: Drop `SupportsIncremental` from this story's edit-set and the contract suite. Re-introduce it in the story that adds the first incremental-fetch path, when there is a real JiraBackend method body and a consumer guard to test against.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add apply_inbound.py (line 492 — `_map_local_to_jira_fields` call) to the enumerated consumers of OutboundMapper so S4's edit-set is complete.
evidence: apply_inbound.py:49: `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import _map_local_to_jira_fields`
apply_inbound.py:492: `fields = _map_local_to_jira_fields(`
Plan states OutboundMapper ← `outbound_fields._map_local_to_jira_fields` but does not list apply_inbound.py as a consumer
findings.finding: apply_inbound.py (line 492) calls `_map_local_to_jira_fields` directly — it is a consumer of the function the plan assigns to OutboundMapper — but it is not listed in the plan's consumer inventory. Since S4 will route OutboundMapper through JiraBackend, apply_inbound.py must also be updated at that time. The plan's Scope section only acknowledges 'outbound_differ.py' and 'dispatch_one.py' implicitly, leaving apply_inbound.py invisible to the S4 author.
findings.location: Approach section — 'OutboundMapper' bullet; apply_inbound.py:492
scenarios: S4 rewires OutboundMapper and audits outbound_differ.py for _map_local_to_jira_fields call sites but misses apply_inbound.py, leaving it calling the unwrapped function and bypassing the adapter seam.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section under the OutboundMapper bullet, append: '(also consumed by apply_inbound.py:492 — must be rewired in S4)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale statement explaining why the five-role Protocol decomposition is the right boundary (e.g., roles are independently substitutable for testing, capability Protocols require role isolation for `isinstance` dispatch, or future backends may compose roles from separate objects), and clarify whether `Backend` is itself a structural Protocol aggregating all role methods or a dataclass/composite holding role-object references.
evidence: Plan describes what the Protocols are ('TicketTransport ← the create/read/update/transition/comment methods', etc.) without stating why sub-role decomposition is chosen over a single Backend Protocol.

ABSENCE: no statement explaining why the five-role split is the right interface boundary (e.g., 'roles are independently testable', 'a future backend may compose roles from separate objects', 'capability detection requires role isolation').
findings.finding: The plan describes the role-Protocol decomposition (five sub-Protocols + three capability Protocols behind one Backend facade) as a design fact but offers no positive rationale for why this decomposition boundary is correct — specifically, why the roles are separated at this granularity rather than as a single flat `Backend` Protocol with all methods.
findings.location: Context/Problem section — '_backend.py' edit-set bullet; Approach section — 'Derive each Protocol's method signatures'
scenarios: An implementer writing JiraBackend uncertain whether to make it a single class implementing all methods (treating sub-Protocols as documentation only) vs. composing separate role objects — the plan doesn't state which composition model Backend enforces.

findings.finding: The three opt-in capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) are named and the contract tests are described as 'exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior)', but the DETECTION MECHANISM — the decision rule by which a caller (or the contract suite) determines whether a given Backend instance advertises an opt-in capability — is never specified anywhere in the plan. The plan does not state whether detection uses isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks), hasattr, a capabilities() method on Backend, or some other protocol. This decision rule must be co-located with the _backend.py schema definition; without it, an implementer of FakeBackend and a reviewer of the contract suite cannot know what 'observed via behavior' actually means at the code level.
findings.location: Testing section — 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links), not via structural introspection'; AC line 2 (contract suite); _backend.py edit-set
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the _backend.py edit-set description: 'Callers detect opt-in capability via isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks) / isinstance(backend, SupportsComments) / isinstance(backend, SupportsIncremental); no separate capabilities() method is needed.' Then note in the contract suite section that FakeBackend selectively inherits the opt-in Protocol to test the guarded path, and omits it to test the skip path.

findings.finding: The plan states `IdentityConvention ← the rebar-id label read/write convention, expressed over RemoteRef`, but unlike the other four Protocols (which delegate to a single existing function), the rebar-id label stamping/finding behavior is distributed across at least four core modules (`dispatch_one.py:320`, `binding_store.py:516,522`, `apply_inbound_records.py:223`, `binding_walk.py:341`). No single wrappable function exists for JiraBackend to delegate to for `IdentityConvention`, making the 'pure delegation shim' claim for this Protocol unverifiable without additional design work.
findings.location: Approach section: 'IdentityConvention ← the rebar-id label read/write convention, expressed over RemoteRef'; also Testing section: 'IdentityConvention stamps and finds a RemoteRef'

findings.finding: The plan attributes `InboundMapper` to `'the inbound_fields/inbound_translate payload→local extraction'`, but `inbound_translate` is primarily an applier-path event-write module (it writes local ticket event files) — not a mapper. Its translation helpers (`_jira_status_to_local`, `_jira_key_to_local_id`, `_resolve_priority`) are used by the inbound applier, not by the differ's mapper. The actual differ-level mapper (`_map_jira_to_local_fields`) lives entirely in `inbound_fields.py`. Conflating `inbound_translate` as an `InboundMapper` source may cause the Protocol's method signatures to include applier-path I/O functions that belong to a different role (or no Protocol at all at this story's scope).
findings.location: Approach section: 'InboundMapper ← the inbound_fields/inbound_translate payload→local extraction'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Approach bullet to: 'InboundMapper ← `inbound_fields._map_jira_to_local_fields` (the pure Jira→local field mapper used by the inbound differ)'; explicitly exclude `inbound_translate` I/O helpers from this Protocol.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
